**CYTOGNOSIS FOUNDATION**

**All Things — Tools**

Consolidated platform tooling map

*Harmonization of 19 internal research tabs into one component view, with recommended stack, decision trees, maturity heatmap, and phased roadmap.*

# **30-second TL;DR**

> **Status**: Active
> **Date**: 2026-07-10
> **Author**: @shahin
> **Audience**: engineers
> **Tags**: `engineering`
> **Variants**: Technical (this doc) - Readable (Obsidian twin optional, same filename) - Agent (n/a)

Cytognosis tooling research spans 19 internal tabs. This document harmonizes them into 11 component groups arranged across three product surfaces: Cytonome (the on-device navigator), Cytoverse (the AI map), and a Foundation Spine (FAIR, lineage, repos). Every primary pick is permissively licensed (Apache 2.0, MIT, or BSD-3) and aligned with the Cytognosis open-science mission.

**If you read nothing else, read these seven decisions:**

* Phone interviewer: Flutter 3.41 \+ Rive \+ Gemma 4 E2B via LiteRT-LM (native audio).  
* Desktop and web dashboard: React 19 \+ Vite \+ Tauri v2 \+ shadcn/ui.  
* Multi-agent runtime: Dapr Agents \+ Google ADK \+ LangGraph, with mDNS / NATS / Tailscale / Iroh CRDT.  
* Knowledge graph: LinkML \+ Biolink Model \+ BioCypher → Neo4j, with LaminDB \+ bionty for lineage.  
* Document pipeline: Docling \+ GROBID \+ spaCy/scispacy \+ Quarto \+ docxtpl \+ Pydantic \+ Instructor.  
* Reproducibility: redun \+ Aim (or MLflow) \+ Zoekt \+ GitNexus \+ auto RO-Crate \+ FAIRSCAPE \+ Zenodo \+ SWHID.  
* Genomic vault: TileDB ecosystem (VCF, SOMA, BioImaging, ML) \+ GA4GH VRS for variant identity.

**What changed during harmonization:**

* LlamaParse v1 replaced by Docling (open, local, MCP-ready).  
* SCION rejected as core network layer; LiveKit Agents reduced to optional cloud voice.  
* Sourcegraph replaced by Zoekt \+ GitNexus (Sourcegraph went private Aug 2024).  
* MLflow vs Aim: Aim primary for solo founder; MLflow when team exceeds three.  
* UI question resolved: split phone (Flutter) and dashboard (React \+ Tauri); two surfaces, one product.

# **Executive summary**

Cytognosis Foundation is building a cellular intelligence platform: GPS for Health. The platform integrates Cytoverse (the AI Map of continuous health coordinates), Cytoscope (programmable biosensors), and Cytonome (the on-device causal AI navigator). The 19 internal research tabs in “All Things — Tools” cover every layer of infrastructure these three components depend on. They span schema and ontology design, multi-omic data engineering, multi-agent orchestration, on-device speech AI, document and grant automation, reproducible research, and front-end engineering.

This consolidated document does three things. First, it reorganizes the 19 tabs into a single 11-component taxonomy. Second, it picks one primary tool per component, with licenses and alternatives, and visualizes maturity, openness, FAIR fit, and Cytognosis fit on a heatmap. Third, it stitches the five overlapping phased roadmaps from the source tabs into one parallel-track plan running from T0 through M24.

| Why this matters.  The original tabs were authored as independent deep dives. They contradict each other in small ways (LlamaParse, MLflow vs Aim, LiveKit, MAF vs Dapr, UI unified vs split, Sourcegraph). Harmonization resolves those tensions, surfaces hidden dependencies (Layer 3 silently constraining Layer 2 in Cytonome), and makes the next wave of decisions (privacy boundary schema, FDA classification, identity provider) visible. |
| :---- |

**Source coverage:**

Source document: All Things — Tools, Google Doc 1yF-zW-TE6CbYfI8JGFJUvNOBKd-OAwhc3S09e8NqBwc, approximately 491,000 characters across 19 distinct tabs and \~7,500 lines, last extracted 2026-04-26. Roughly 250 individual tools and standards are referenced across the tabs; this document presents the recommended subset with rationale, alternatives, and a complete inventory in the appendix.

# **1\. Unified component architecture**

All 19 tabs map onto 11 component groups arranged across three surfaces. Cytonome, Cytoverse, and the Foundation Spine. The schema-as-hub pattern (LinkML threading through doc generation, the knowledge graph, lineage, and grant templating) and the MCP fabric (Model Context Protocol exposing every component to agents) are the two lateral connectors that make the architecture coherent rather than a list.

![][image1]

*Figure 1\. Cytognosis platform component map. 11 component groups, three surfaces, two lateral connectors (LinkML schema spine and MCP fabric).*

**How to read this map:**

* Top band (Cytonome) is the on-device, voice-driven product surface. UI, voice agent, and multi-agent orchestration sit here.  
* Middle band (Cytoverse) is the AI map: knowledge graph, multi-omic data lake. Cytoscope (sensors) is acknowledged but out of tooling-doc scope.  
* Bottom band (Foundation Spine) is what every component depends on: standards, document pipelines, model serialization, lineage, and the external repos index.  
* The dashed vertical between bands is conceptual: LinkML schemas authored in the Spine flow up into Cytoverse as the knowledge graph, then into Cytonome as the agent context.

# **3\. Tool maturity, openness, FAIR fit, and Cytognosis fit**

Every tool referenced in the recommended stack and its alternatives is scored 1-5 on four axes: openness of license and source, maturity of implementation and documentation, FAIR fit (does it support FAIR data and metadata practices natively), and Cytognosis fit (does it match the platform's three-surface architecture and open-source posture). Status column tags primary picks, alternatives, and dropped tools.

![][image3]

*Figure 3\. Maturity heatmap across 70+ tools. Higher score is better. Status tags: Pick, Alt, Drop, Defer, Closed.*

| How to use this.  This heatmap is the fastest way to spot upgrade paths. A row that is mostly 5 with a 3 in Maturity is a tool worth watching; a row that is 5 in Cytognosis fit but 2 in Openness is a tool to replace (e.g., Sourcegraph replaced by Zoekt \+ GitNexus). Use it together with Figure 2 when communicating with stakeholders who want quantitative justifications. |

# **5\. Consolidated phased roadmap**

Five overlapping roadmaps appear in the source tabs (multi-agent MVP at 12 weeks, voice AI at 24 months, Cytonome cross-platform at 8-10 months, reproducible research sandbox at 6 weeks, Cytoverse foundation work). They run on parallel tracks. This single timeline shows how they interlock against four critical milestones.

![][image9]

*Figure 9\. Consolidated phased roadmap, six parallel tracks, four critical red milestones.*

**Critical interlocks:**

* M5 end-to-end interview demo gates everything downstream. The Cytonome MVP track must hit it before voice AI Phase 2 can start clinical evaluation.  
* M6 FDA self-classification (preventive/behavioral vs diagnostic) determines whether MedGemma stays feature-flagged or has to be replaced.  
* M9 pilot deploy (100-500 users) is the first time the Foundation Spine, Cytonome, and Voice AI tracks meet the same users at the same time.  
* M18 Cytoverse v1.0 (10+ disorders, GraphQL API) is what the supervisor LLM queries through MCP for clinical context.

# **6\. UI / Cross-Platform Front-End (Cytonome shell)**

**Surface: Cytonome**

| Primary pick | Flutter 3.41 \+ Rive (mobile); React 19 \+ Vite \+ Tauri v2 \+ shadcn/ui (desktop+web) |
| :---- | :---- |
| **License** | BSD-3 (Flutter); MIT/Apache 2.0 (React, Vite, Tauri, shadcn) |
| **Source tabs** | Tabs 12 (Cytonome Architecture), 18 (Cross-Platform Framework Decision) |
| **Strong alternatives** | React Native \+ Expo, Kotlin Multiplatform \+ Compose, Compose Multiplatform Desktop, Electron |
| **Status** | Decided. Two surfaces, one product. |

**TL;DR (30s).**

The phone (interviewer plus persona) and the dashboard (analytics plus configuration) are different products that happen to share data. Pick the framework that maximizes each one's strengths instead of forcing unification. Flutter wins the phone because Rive plus the Impeller renderer give pixel-perfect persona animation across platforms; React with Tauri wins the dashboard because the JavaScript ecosystem owns the data-viz libraries (Recharts, AG Grid, React Flow, TanStack), and Tauri ships a 600 KB binary that runs everywhere.

**Why Flutter for the phone.**

* Impeller renderer guarantees identical UI across iOS and Android, which is essential for the persona on which therapeutic alliance depends.  
* Rive integration is mature and is the only production-grade vector animation pipeline with a Flutter-first runtime.  
* LiteRT-LM bindings via platform channels expose Gemma 4 E2B with native audio input on-device.  
* Native audio input collapses what would otherwise be a three-stage pipeline (capture, Whisper STT, LLM reasoning) into a single model call.

**Why React \+ Tauri for desktop and web.**

* JavaScript dashboard libraries (Recharts, Apache ECharts, D3, Observable Plot, TanStack Table, AG Grid, React Flow, Cytoscape.js) are the reason every data product ends up here.  
* Tauri v2 ships a 600 KB hello-world binary, 30x lighter than Electron's 150 MB, with sub-500 ms startup.  
* Tauri sidecar pattern lets the supervisor LLM (Python, Google ADK, Gemma 4 26B MoE) run as a managed local subprocess; same OpenAPI contract as the cloud fallback.  
* Same React app deploys to web (PWA) and desktop without code changes.

**How the two surfaces stay coherent.**

* OpenAPI 3.1 codegen targets both Dart (mobile) and TypeScript (desktop). Single source of truth for every API response.  
* Style Dictionary design tokens compile to Flutter ThemeData and Tailwind CSS variables.  
* Single identity provider (Auth0, Clerk, Supabase Auth, or self-hosted Keycloak; decision deferred to M1).  
* Same Gemma 4 model family across phone (E2B), supervisor (26B-A4B), and optional specialist (MedGemma 1.5).

**What was rejected and why.**

| Rejected | Reason |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Electron** | 150 MB binaries vs Tauri's 600 KB. RAM idle 200-300 MB vs Tauri's 30-40 MB. |
| **React Native \+ Expo** | Native widget drift breaks brand parity; weaker animation than Flutter \+ Rive. |
| **Kotlin Multiplatform \+ Compose iOS** | Compose iOS still maturing in 2026; thinner AI/ML and animation libs. |
| **Flutter Desktop** | Dashboard library ecosystem trails JS (no Recharts/AG Grid/React Flow equivalent). |
| **.NET MAUI / Uno Platform** | Microsoft tail risk; small dev pool; no advantage for non-MS shop. |
| **Qt 6.x** | LGPL compliance burden; loses web target. |
| **NativeScript, Ionic/Capacitor, Cordova** | Declining or superseded ecosystems. |

| Open question.  Persona concept validation gates the timeline. Schedule a low-fi proto user-test by M2 to lock direction before Flutter and Rive build investment compounds. |
| :---- |

# **7\. Empathic Voice Agent (Interviewer)**

**Surface: Cytonome**

| Primary pick | Phase 1: Whisper \+ Gemma 4 E4B \+ Kokoro (Architecture D). Phase 3: Migrate edge to Gemma 4 cascaded (Architecture A). |
| :---- | :---- |
| **License** | Apache 2.0 (Gemma 4, Whisper, Kokoro) |
| **Source tabs** | Tabs 12, 13 |
| **Strong alternatives** | Architecture B (Moshi 7B \+ Qwen3-Omni 30B-A3B); Architecture C (LFM2.5-Audio-1.5B \+ Gemma 4 26B) |
| **Status** | Default path picked. Crisis detection and paralinguistic sensors are model-agnostic and required from Phase 1\. |

**TL;DR (30s).**

No open speech-to-speech model scores above 8/10 on all seven of Cytognosis's hard requirements at once (sub-300 ms latency, user barge-in, supervisor barge-in, native paralinguistic, open weights, edge-only inference, multi-agent collaboration). Pick by phase: start with Architecture D (Whisper \+ Gemma 4 \+ Kokoro) for the lowest-risk pilot, migrate to Architecture A (fully Gemma 4 cascaded) at M15-21 once the model family proves out, and only consider Architecture B (Moshi) at M21+ if naturalism research justifies the CC-BY licensing complexity.

**Hard requirements (from Tab 13, applied to every architecture).**

* Sub-300 ms first-audio latency; 600-800 ms acceptable for Phase 1\.  
* User barge-in (interrupt the model mid-utterance).  
* Supervisor barge-in (cloud supervisor can intervene in phone session).  
* Native paralinguistic processing (tone, pace, prosody, filled pauses).  
* Open weights with permissive license. Hume EVI and Sesame CSM are closed; benchmark refs only.  
* Edge-only inference for the interviewer (PHI never leaves device).  
* Multi-agent collaboration (interviewer plus supervisor plus optional specialist).

**Architecture comparison.**

| Arch | Edge | Cloud | License | Latency e2e | Best for |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **A: Gemma 4 cascaded** | Gemma 4 E4B \+ Kokoro | Gemma 4 26B-A4B | Apache 2.0 | 500-700 ms | Multilingual, unified family |
| **B: Moshi \+ Qwen3-Omni** | Moshi 7B | Qwen3-Omni 30B-A3B | CC-BY 4.0 \+ Apache 2.0 | 200-300 ms | Naturalism-first |
| **C: LFM2.5-Audio \+ Gemma 4** | LFM2.5-Audio-1.5B | Gemma 4 26B-A4B | LFM Open \+ Apache 2.0 | 300-500 ms | Smallest edge |
| **D: Whisper \+ Gemma 4 \+ Kokoro** | Whisper \+ Gemma 4 E4B \+ Kokoro | Gemma 4 26B-A4B | Apache 2.0 | 600-800 ms | Phase 1 pilot, lowest risk |

**Critical gaps that must be built separately.**

* Paralinguistic sensor (CRITICAL): no open S2S has therapy-grade benchmarks. Build parallel sensor with HuBERT-large SER, openSMILE eGeMAPSv02, jitter/shimmer/HNR features, filled-pause detection. Estimated 3-6 months.  
* Crisis detection (CRITICAL): no model has built-in suicide or self-harm classification. Build hard-coded keyword rules \+ multi-modal classifier \+ human review queue \+ warm handoff \+ 988 hotline integration. Estimated 6-12 months. Cannot defer past Phase 1\.  
* Multilingual audio (MODERATE): Architecture A or D for \>35 languages.  
* Long-form audio (MODERATE): Gemma 4 caps at 30s clips; therapy is 45-60 min. Implement rolling chunking with 5s overlap and stitched context buffer.

| Why phasing order matters.  Crisis detection and paralinguistic sensors are model-agnostic. Building them in Phase 1 pays off across every subsequent phase, regardless of which architecture wins. Defer them and the entire 24-month roadmap blocks at the first IRB review. |
| :---- |

**Go/no-go checklist (proceed only if all true).**

* 8-12 engineers committed for 12-24 months.  
* $1.5-3M budget for Phases 1-3.  
* Clinical advisor partnership signed.  
* 24/7 human clinician on-call infrastructure.  
* HIPAA cloud BAA in place.  
* IRB review approved.  
* Dedicated crisis detection subsystem owner.

# **8\. Multi-Agent Orchestration & Discovery**

**Surface: Cytonome**

| Primary pick | Dapr Agents \+ Google ADK \+ LangGraph; mDNS / DNS-SD; NATS JetStream; Tailscale (or Nebula); Iroh Documents (CRDT) |
| :---- | :---- |
| **License** | Apache 2.0 / MIT / BSD-3 / WireGuard for cloud transport |
| **Source tabs** | Tabs 11, 12, 17 |
| **Strong alternatives** | Architecture B (libp2p \+ Iroh \+ Naptha, pure P2P); Architecture C (Ray \+ Dapr \+ QUIC \+ ExecuTorch, cloud-native) |
| **Status** | Architecture A picked. SCION rejected. LiveKit reduced to optional cloud voice fallback. |

**TL;DR (30s).**

No single framework satisfies all eight Cytonome requirements (HIPAA, zero-config discovery, reliable session handshake, sub-millisecond LAN latency, distributed state, open-source, mobile-native, supervisor never drops). The answer is a hybrid stack of 5-6 complementary technologies. Discovery should be DHCP-like (zero-config on LAN); connections should be TCP-like (handshake before session starts, no drops mid-conversation). Centralize PHI (HIPAA right-to-be-forgotten conflicts with public distributed storage like IPFS or Autonomi).

**Tier responsibilities and traffic split (target).**

| Tier | Role | Traffic share | Latency target |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Tier 1 (Phone, 3B model)** | On-device, edge-only inference, primary interviewer | 90% queries | \<1s P95 |
| **Tier 2 (Laptop or local supervisor, 7-26B)** | Session supervision, guidance, dual-model eval | 8% queries | 1-2s P95 |
| **Tier 3 (Cloud, Claude/GPT-4 fallback)** | Heaviest reasoning, fallback only | 2% queries | 2-5s P95 |

**Why each layer was picked.**

* Dapr Agents (Apache 2.0): virtual actors, language-agnostic sidecar; wins over MAF on Orleans because Flutter mobile pick rules out MAUI peer-agent advantage.  
* Google ADK: orchestration logic on top of Dapr; native function calling; same toolchain as Vertex AI.  
* LangGraph: stateful agent graph for the supervisor; mature checkpointing; Apache 2.0.  
* mDNS / DNS-SD: only true zero-config LAN discovery. Service \`\_cytonome-agent.\_tcp\` with TXT carrying role, model, dapr\_app\_id, and mcp\_tools. Implementations: bonsoir (Flutter), mdns-sd (Rust).  
* NATS JetStream: sub-millisecond LAN pub/sub; single 20 MB binary; persistent durable subjects.  
* Tailscale (BSD-3 client) or Nebula (Slack-built, MIT, full OSS): WireGuard-based tunnels with device identity. Headscale for self-hosted Tailscale control plane.  
* Iroh Documents (CRDT, MIT): mobile-ready distributed state for non-PHI metadata and agent state. PHI never goes here.  
* Application-layer 3-tier HA: primary laptop \+ cloud hot-standby \+ on-phone degraded mode at 5s lapse. "Supervisor cannot drop" is not delivered by any framework, so Cytognosis owns the pattern.

**What was rejected and why.**

| Rejected | Reason |
| :---- | :---- |
| **SCION** | Wrong abstraction layer (inter-domain ISP routing). No mobile SDK, no discovery, fatal R4 failure. "Like buying an 18-wheeler for your daily commute." |
| **LiveKit Agents (as core)** | Server-side-only agent model; centralized hub-and-spoke contradicts distributed P2P. Phones become passive WebRTC clients. |
| **LiveKit Agents (as cloud voice fallback only)** | ACCEPTED for Architecture A.2 when telephony or web-based voice sessions are needed. |
| **Microsoft Agent Framework on Orleans \+ MAUI** | Incompatible with Flutter mobile pick. Layer 3 silently constrains Layer 2\. |
| **AgentScope 1.0** | Weak HA primitives. |
| **Google Scion** | Experimental. |
| **LangGraph as supervisor (Tab 11\) vs Dapr Agents (Tab 12\)** | Refined: LangGraph stays for agent graph logic; Dapr handles distributed runtime. |
| **AutoGen / AG2 / CrewAI / Letta / OpenAI Swarm** | Single-process, wrong tier. |
| **Pure P2P (libp2p \+ Iroh \+ Naptha)** | 5-6 month build vs 10-12 weeks; HIPAA-hostile. |
| **Crypto-dependent agents (Fetch.ai uAgents, Olas, Bittensor, Morpheus)** | Cryptocurrency dependency unacceptable for healthcare. |

**Privacy boundary (must be schema-enforced before implementation).**

* Phone-only (never crosses wire): raw audio, full transcripts, raw feature vectors, biometric markers.  
* Crosses wire (schema-enforced filtering): high-level events (stress\_signal, topic\_shift, user\_disengaged), structured summaries (session phase, mood arc), supervisor commands (guidance hints, interrupt signals).

| Open: privacy boundary as code.  The privacy boundary is currently a verbal agreement. It must become a LinkML-validated schema with CI gates blocking any new field that lands on the wire side without an explicit redaction policy. This precedes Phase 1 implementation. |
| :---- |

# **9\. Knowledge Graph Stack**

**Surface: Cytoverse**

| Primary pick | LinkML \+ Biolink Model \+ BioCypher → Neo4j; LaminDB \+ bionty for lineage; OAK \+ DuckDB \+ Parquet for analytics |
| :---- | :---- |
| **License** | Apache 2.0 / MIT / BSD-3 |
| **Source tabs** | Tabs 6, 7, 14, 19 |
| **Strong alternatives** | Direct Triple Store (Apache Jena, Blazegraph) \+ SPARQL; SemOpenAlex pattern (RDF triples \+ SPARQL endpoint) |
| **Status** | Decided. LinkML is the schema hub; everything else compiles from it. |

**TL;DR (30s).**

LinkML is the schema hub. Every other artifact (Pydantic models, JSON-Schema, GraphQL, OWL, ShEx, JSON-LD, SHACL, SQL DDL, Markdown stubs, TypeScript types) compiles from LinkML YAML. Biolink Model is itself written in LinkML and provides the biomedical entity types. BioCypher consumes LinkML schemas and Python adapters to bulk-import Neo4j. LaminDB plus bionty provides lineage and ontology validation. OAK plus Semantic-SQL on SQLite handles transactional ontology access; KGX TSV plus DuckDB plus Parquet handles analytics (50x faster than SQLite for aggregations).

**The schema hub idea.**

* Author the model once in LinkML YAML.  
* Compile to every downstream representation via gen-python, gen-pydantic, gen-json-schema, gen-typescript, gen-owl, gen-shacl, gen-shex, gen-sqla, gen-graphql, gen-markdown.  
* Validate against the YAML; fix once, fix everywhere.  
* LinkML-Map adds declarative source-to-target mapping (height\_in\_cm → height\_in\_meters). Compiles to Python or DuckDB SQL.  
* Schema-Automator infers LinkML from existing CSV/JSON/SQL when retrofitting legacy data.

**BioCypher's three-layer model (Tab 14).**

* Adapters: Python generators yielding (subject, predicate, object) tuples from any source.  
* schema\_config.yaml: the central contract between adapters and Neo4j; reification via represented\_as: node; explicit is\_a subclassing.  
* Ontology integration: LinkML schemas grounded in Biolink \+ OBO Foundry (HPO, MONDO, UBERON, CL).  
* Native Neo4j bulk-import; integrates with Open Targets via MCP; LaminDB → BioCypher adapter is a 2-3 week build.

**Identifier and mapping harmonization.**

* Bioregistry (Biopragmatics): meta-registry of CURIEs and prefixes; resolves provider drift.  
* NCATS NodeNormalization: runtime CURIE merge; pairs with Bioregistry.  
* SSSOM: Simple Standard for Sharing Ontological Mappings (skos:exactMatch / broadMatch with curator and confidence).  
* SeMRA: mapping reasoner with Inversion, Mutation, and Transitivity operations.  
* UMLS: stored as SSSOM tables, never imported as graph (avoids bloat).  
* SNOMED CT → ELK reasoner → KGX for licensed clinical terminology integration.

**Storage split: transactional vs analytical.**

| Stage | Pick | When |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Transactional** | OAK \+ Semantic-SQL on SQLite | Ontology API, single-record fetch, term lookup |
| **Analytical** | KGX TSV \+ Parquet \+ DuckDB | Aggregations across millions of edges, 50x faster than SQLite |
| **Graph DB** | Neo4j (community) | Cypher queries from BioChatter; pattern matching |
| **Lineage** | LaminDB \+ bionty | Artifact provenance, ontology validation, links to redun runs |
| **Bibliometric** | SemOpenAlex SPARQL endpoint (Metaphacts) | 26B RDF triples derived from OpenAlex; embeds DistMult |

| MCP fabric for the KG.  BioContextAI exposes the entire knowledge graph stack via MCP servers (knowledgebase-mcp, anndata-mcp, mcp-server-cookiecutter). Combined with BioChatter (natural language to Cypher) and LangChain's langchain-mcp-adapters, the supervisor LLM can query Neo4j, OpenAlex, Open Targets, and the local LaminDB without bespoke wiring per source. |
| :---- |

# **10\. Standards, Ontologies, FAIR**

**Surface: Spine**

| Primary pick | LinkML schemas; GA4GH (VRS, Cat-VRS, Phenopackets, DRS); OBO (HPO, MONDO, UBERON, CL, EFO); SSSOM \+ Bioregistry; W3C WADM; RO-Crate (Workflow Run profile); SWHID; CITATION.cff |
| :---- | :---- |
| **License** | Various open (CC0, CC BY, Apache 2.0) |
| **Source tabs** | Tabs 1, 6, 7, 8, 14, 16 |
| **Strong alternatives** | Custom JSON Schema \+ bespoke registry (rejected — no ontology grounding, no SemWeb interop) |
| **Status** | Decided. RO-Crate is operational, not aspirational. |

**TL;DR (30s).**

Cytognosis adopts open scientific standards as a matter of operating practice, not as a publication-time checkbox. RO-Crate is auto-emitted from every workflow run. SWHID, DOI, and ARK identifiers cover code, data, and provenance. LinkML threads the schema layer; GA4GH covers genomic interoperability; OBO Foundry covers phenotype and anatomy; SSSOM and Bioregistry resolve identifier drift; W3C WADM covers annotation; Schema.org plus Bioschemas cover dataset discoverability. Every standard listed here has shipping implementations and live ecosystem support in 2026\.

**Standards by domain.**

| Domain | Standard | Why |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Schema authoring** | LinkML | YAML source-of-truth, compiles to all downstream forms |
| **Biomedical entities** | Biolink Model v4.3.9 | LinkML-authored; KG node and edge types |
| **Phenotype, anatomy** | OBO Foundry (HPO, MONDO, UBERON, CL, UPHENO) | Open ontologies, ROBOT for tooling |
| **Phenotype-aware datasets** | EFO, RDoC, HiTOP | EFO for trait-level GWAS; RDoC \+ HiTOP for psychiatric phenotyping |
| **Genomic variant identity** | GA4GH VRS, Cat-VRS, VA-Spec | Cryptographic hashes replace fragile rsIDs |
| **Genomic exchange** | GA4GH Phenopackets, DRS, TRS, Passports | Federated exchange standards |
| **Identifier resolution** | Bioregistry, NCATS NodeNormalization | Meta-registry \+ runtime CURIE merge |
| **Mapping format** | SSSOM (skos:exactMatch \+ curator \+ confidence) | Schema for cross-ontology mappings |
| **Mapping reasoning** | SeMRA (Inversion, Mutation, Transitivity) | Operations on SSSOM tables |
| **Annotation** | W3C Web Annotation Data Model (WADM) | Hypothes.is is the gold-standard implementation |
| **FAIR packaging** | RO-Crate (Process / Workflow / Provenance Run Crate) | Auto-emit from redun every run; FAIRSCAPE for ARK \+ governance |
| **Code archival** | SWHID (ISO 18670:2025), Software Heritage | Permanent, content-addressed code identifier |
| **Citation metadata** | CITATION.cff | Mandatory in every Cytognosis repo |
| **Workflow run** | WRROC (Workflow Run RO-Crate) profiles, Five Safes | Process Run Crate (MVP), Workflow Run Crate, Provenance Run Crate, Five Safes for TRE-FX |
| **Discoverability** | Bioschemas \+ Schema.org | Markup via Data Discovery Engine (DDE) by Scripps/BioThings |
| **Semantic Web** | JSON-LD, RDF, Turtle, OWL, SHACL, ShEx | All compile from LinkML |
| **Agent protocols** | MCP (de facto standard) \+ A2A; ACP, ANP, LMOS (watch) | BioContextAI is the biomedical MCP registry |
| **Model serialization** | Safetensors (security), GGUF (edge), ONNX (bridge) | See Section 12 |

**FAIR maturity in 2026\.**

* Widely adopted (no caveats): FAIR principles, DOI/CrossRef, Git SHA, BIDS, Parquet, ONNX, Schema.org, JSON-LD.  
* Rapidly growing, safe to adopt early: SWHID (ISO 18670:2025), CITATION.cff, SafeTensors, RO-Crate, Model Cards, LaminDB (production at Pfizer, Altos, DZNE), Quarto.  
* Specialized but mature in domain: GGUF (53.5% of HuggingFace LLMs), TileDB, Apache Hudi, Zarr.  
* Emerging — adopt with caution: FAIR Digital Objects (sparse implementations), full RDF/OWL (high complexity), IPFS for research (mostly Web3), Git SHA-256 (GitLab and Codeberg yes; GitHub no as of 2026), Five Safes RO-Crate.

| DMP master, four extracts.  Maintain a single 7-section FAIR-organized Data Management Plan as the canonical document. Extract funder-specific submissions from it: NIH (new 7-element format mandatory May 25, 2026, NOT-OD-26-046, 2-page limit), ARPA-H (flexible, milestone-driven), NSF Tech Labs (Research.gov DMP tool launches April 27, 2026, 5 components within 2 pages), EU Horizon Europe ("as open as possible, as closed as necessary"; full DMP by Month 6; CC0 metadata mandatory; Zenodo recommended). |
| :---- |

# **11\. Document Generation & Extraction**

**Surface: Spine**

| Primary pick | Docling (default ingestion) \+ GROBID (scientific PDFs) \+ spaCy/scispacy (NER) \+ Quarto+Typst \+ docxtpl \+ openpyxl+Jinja \+ Pydantic \+ Instructor |
| :---- | :---- |
| **License** | MIT / Apache 2.0 |
| **Source tabs** | Tabs 2, 3, 4, 5 |
| **Strong alternatives** | Unstructured.io (enterprise), MyST (Jupyter alt to Quarto), Marker (deferred, RAIL-M license risk) |
| **Status** | V2 stack adopted. LlamaParse v1 retired; PyMuPDF avoided (AGPL). |

**TL;DR (30s).**

We fill templates; we don't generate from zero. The schema is the hub, not the template. The full pipeline reads: Docling or GROBID parses input, spaCy plus scispacy enriches with biomedical entities, Pydantic plus Instructor structures the LLM fill, LinkML validates the output, then Quarto plus Typst renders narrative PDFs while docxtpl renders DOCX templates and openpyxl plus Jinja renders XLSX budgets. LibreOffice headless plus Pandoc plus Tesseract serve as the universal utility layer. Both Anthropic's skills and K-Dense's converge on the same small handful of libraries, which is the commodity office-I/O stack.

**What changed from v1 to v2 of the templating stack.**

| Layer | v1 (March 2026\) | v2 (April 2026, current) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Ingestion** | LlamaParse v2 (paid, cloud) | Docling (IBM, MIT, local, MCP-ready) |
| **Specialist parser** | — | GROBID (scientific PDFs, \>0.90 F1) |
| **LLM-context shim** | — | markitdown (Microsoft, lossy, fast) |
| **NER enrichment** | — | spaCy \+ scispacy (UMLS, MeSH, RxNorm linking) |
| **DOCX templating** | docxtpl (kept) | docxtpl (kept) |
| **XLSX templating** | openpyxl \+ Jinja (kept) | openpyxl \+ Jinja \+ LibreOffice recalc.py |
| **PDF/HTML/MD narrative** | Quarto | Quarto \+ Typst backend (instant compile) |
| **Schema validation** | LinkML | LinkML (kept) |
| **LLM structured fill** | Pydantic \+ Instructor | Pydantic \+ Instructor (kept) |
| **Universal utilities** | Pandoc, LibreOffice, Tesseract | Pandoc, LibreOffice, Tesseract, qpdf, pypdf (kept) |

**Why Docling beat LlamaParse.**

* MIT license vs commercial SaaS subscription.  
* Local execution vs upload to LlamaIndex cloud.  
* MCP server included; LlamaParse does not.  
* Format coverage: PDF, DOCX, PPTX, XLSX, HTML, LaTeX, audio, images.  
* First-run VLM downloads 1-3 GB; budget for that.  
* Structured-extraction is beta; wrap with Instructor \+ Pydantic for the last mile.  
* Chemistry structures (SMILES) not supported by Docling; if needed, add a Marker pipeline behind a feature flag.

**Decision rules at a glance.**

* Scientific paper with citations: GROBID (TEI/XML output, 0.90+ F1, 10+ docs/sec).  
* Scanned PDF without text layer: Tesseract \+ pdf2image fallback when Docling OCR fails.  
* Need fast lossy LLM context: markitdown.  
* Default for everything else: Docling.  
* Need cell formula recalc in DOCX or XLSX template fills: Anthropic skill recalc.py (LibreOffice-backed).  
* Funder DOCX without templating: Anthropic unpack.py / pack.py (XML round-trip).

**Migration plan (Tab 3).**

* Week 1: install Docling \+ markitdown; A/B vs LlamaParse on representative funder PDFs.  
* Week 2: stand up Dockerized GROBID; pipe PI publication corpus.  
* Week 3: spaCy \+ scispacy \+ en\_core\_sci\_lg \+ en\_ner\_bc5cdr\_md.  
* Week 4: port recalc.py from Anthropic skill.  
* Week 5: adopt unpack.py / pack.py fallback for non-templated DOCX.

| Schema is the hub, not the template.  Author content keyed to LinkML classes (Project, Aim, Heilmeier\_Question, Budget\_Line). Render-time templates are dumb sinks. This is what makes one source of truth survive ARPA-H, NIH, NSF Tech Labs, and EU Horizon variants without copy-paste drift. |
| :---- |

# **12\. AI Artifact Storage & Serialization**

**Surface: Spine**

| Primary pick | Safetensors (public weights) \+ GGUF (edge LLM) \+ ONNX (cross-platform) \+ LiteRT (mobile NPU) \+ ExecuTorch (PyTorch mobile) \+ MLX (Apple Silicon) \+ Keras v3 (authoring) \+ Orbax (training) |
| :---- | :---- |
| **License** | Apache 2.0 / MIT (all) |
| **Source tabs** | Tabs 9, 10 |
| **Strong alternatives** | TorchScript (legacy), Msgpack/Flax (JAX research) |
| **Status** | Lifecycle-dependent. The naive monolithic format is gone. |

**TL;DR (30s).**

There is no longer a single AI artifact format. Pick by lifecycle phase. Safetensors replaces Pickle for public weight distribution because Pickle has demonstrated arbitrary code execution attacks (Sleepy Pickle achieved 89-100% scanner evasion in 2023). GGUF dominates edge LLM inference (53.5% of HuggingFace LLMs as of 2026; 70B at Q4\_K\_M fits in 35 GB). ONNX 1.21+ supports 2-bit quantization plus TensorRT, CoreML, QNN, and WebGPU. LiteRT (formerly TF Lite) covers mobile NPUs. ExecuTorch is the PyTorch-native edge format. MLX is Apple Silicon only. Keras v3 is the backend-agnostic authoring format (TF, PyTorch, JAX, OpenVINO). Orbax handles distributed JAX checkpoints with async resharding. StableHLO is the compiler IR with a 5-year backward-compatibility guarantee.

**Format selection by lifecycle phase.**

| Phase | Format | Why |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Public weight distribution** | Safetensors | Zero-copy mmap; no arbitrary code; HuggingFace standard |
| **Local / edge LLM inference** | GGUF / GGUFv3 | llama.cpp, Ollama, LM Studio; Q4\_K\_M for 70B in 35 GB |
| **Cross-platform inference bridge** | ONNX 1.21+ | 2-bit quant; TensorRT, CoreML, QNN, WebGPU |
| **Enterprise serving** | TensorFlow SavedModel | 40% of Fortune 500 still demand TF |
| **Backend-agnostic authoring** | Keras v3 (.keras) | Runs on TF, PyTorch, JAX, OpenVINO |
| **Apple Silicon edge** | MLX | M-series only; tight memory \+ Neural Engine |
| **Mobile NPU** | LiteRT (formerly TF Lite, FlatBuffers) | Cytonome on Flutter via LiteRT-LM |
| **PyTorch mobile** | ExecuTorch (.pte) | Meta-supported; PyTorch-native edge |
| **Distributed training** | Orbax | Async, resharding, JAX/Google; orbax-export bridges to SavedModel |
| **Compiler IR** | StableHLO | MLIR, 5-year backward-compat |
| **Browser** | TensorFlow.js (LayersModel) \+ WebGPU | In-browser inference |
| **Pickle (.pt, .pth)** | OBSOLETE for sharing | Sleepy Pickle attacks; arbitrary code execution |
| **TorchScript** | Legacy / transitional | Use ExecuTorch for new code |
| **Msgpack / Flax** | Specialized | JAX research only |

**Governance and protocol layer (emerging).**

* NIST AI Agent Standards Initiative (CAISI 2026): SCIM \+ NGAC for agent identity.  
* IEEE P2941 family: 2941-2021 (encapsulation), 2941.2-2023 (inference engine APIs), P2941.3 (MoE/instruct-tuning representation).  
* MCP (Model Context Protocol): de facto standard for agent-to-tool integration; BioContextAI registry.  
* A2A: agent-to-agent protocol; ACP, ANP, LMOS are watch-list.

**MLOps deployment (Tab 10).**

* Storage: Google Cloud Storage with versioning (gs://cytognosis-dvc-store).  
* Git/UI hub: DagsHub (links external MLflow at mlflow.cytognosis.org).  
* Data versioning: DVC with HuggingFace import support.  
* Tracking: MLflow 3.4+ with mlflow.autolog(); deploy MLflow MCP Server for AI agents.  
* Architecture viz: Netron as Cloud Run service (ONNX, Safetensors, GGUF).  
* Persistent IDs: Handle System or Cloud Run reverse proxy → https://pid.cytognosis.org/dataset/12345.  
* RO-Crate generation: rocrate Python library post-MLflow training; conforms to FAIR Workflow Run Crate profile.

| Why Pickle is non-negotiable.  Sleepy Pickle attacks demonstrated 89-100% scanner evasion in 2023\. Treating .pt or .pth files from the public internet as executable is closer to truth than treating them as data. Safetensors solves this for distribution; GGUF solves it for edge; ONNX solves it for cross-platform. |
| :---- |

# **13\. Genomic / Multi-Omic Data Stack**

**Surface: Cytoverse**

| Primary pick | TileDB ecosystem (TileDB-VCF, TileDB-SOMA, TileDB-BioImaging, TileDB-ML); CRAM (archival); BCF/bgzipped VCF (transfer); GA4GH VRS (variant identity); Hail VDS (Spark); VCF-Zarr (tensor); LanceDB/SLAF (multimodal) |
| :---- | :---- |
| **License** | MIT / Apache 2.0 |
| **Source tabs** | Tab 8 (Cross-Disorder GWAS Summary Statistics) |
| **Strong alternatives** | GenomicsDB (legacy), PGEN (PLINK2 single-node QC only) |
| **Status** | TileDB ecosystem chosen as central vault. Solves the N+1 problem. |

**TL;DR (30s).**

Stop using flat text files for AI. Shift to multidimensional arrays and semantic graph ontologies to make data tensor-ready. TileDB Ecosystem is the central vault: TileDB-VCF for variants, TileDB-SOMA for single-cell, TileDB-BioImaging for imaging, TileDB-ML for direct PyTorch and TensorFlow streaming. GA4GH VRS replaces fragile rsIDs with cryptographic hashes. BGZIP-compressed Tabix-indexed VCF (and BCF) remains the undisputed universal standard for transfer and interoperability.

**Storage format hierarchy.**

| Stage | First choice | Legacy | Why |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Raw seq** | CRAM | BAM/FASTQ | \>30% size reduction |
| **Single-node QC** | PGEN (PLINK2) | BED/BIM/FAM | Sparse rare variants |
| **Universal transfer** | BCF / bgzipped VCF | TSV | Universal language |
| **Distributed ETL** | Hail VDS / MatrixTable | — | 1M+ samples on Spark |
| **Central DB** | TileDB Ecosystem | GenomicsDB | 3D sparse arrays; eliminates N+1 |
| **AI training** | TileDB-ML / VCF-Zarr / LanceDB | Parquet/TSV | Hundreds-x faster |
| **Variant ID** | GA4GH VRS / Cat-VRS | dbSNP rsIDs | Cryptographic hashes; rsIDs are unstable |

**Phenotype, ontology, harmonization.**

* EFO \+ HPO \+ SNOMED CT: DAG-structured for hierarchical learning.  
* Phenotype mappers: HPO Mapper (\~85% accuracy on unstructured clinical text — known limit).  
* Sumstats harmonization: GWASLab or MungeSumstats.  
* QC: PLINK2 (single-node), Hail (distributed).  
* Imputation: IMPUTE5 \+ GLIMPSE2 (state of art).

**Cytognosis Genetic Intake pipeline (4 stages).**

* Stage 1: multi-format intake → bgzipped VCF / CRAM.  
* Stage 2: liftover GRCh37→GRCh38, allele alignment, vrs-python hashing, VRS/Cat-VRS/VA-Spec wrapping.  
* Stage 3: TileDB-VCF core vault.  
* Stage 4: routes to Hail (Spark), Zarr (sgkit, 300x CPU reduction for tensor-pure DL), LanceDB (multimodal lakehouse), TileDB-ML (direct PyTorch streaming).

**Operational gotchas (from Tab 8).**

* Pan-UKBB stores p-values as natural log; convert before mixing with PGC and ENIGMA sumstats.  
* UKB-RAP enforces compute-to-data; you cannot download individual-level data.  
* HPO Mapper accuracy on free text caps at \~85%; assume manual review on long tail.  
* TileDB-BioImaging does not support BIDS; wrapper is a substantial project (Tab 19).

| Why TileDB.  TileDB Ecosystem is the only stack that lets variant data, single-cell data, imaging data, and ML training data live in the same substrate without N+1 ETL. The point of consolidating to TileDB is that every Cytoverse foundation model can stream from one source of truth instead of a forest of format-specific pipelines. |
| :---- |

# **14\. Reproducible Research, Lineage, Code Intelligence**

**Surface: Spine**

| Primary pick | LaminDB \+ bionty (lineage); redun (compute graph, AST \+ data hash); Aim (solo) or MLflow (team); Zoekt \+ GitNexus (code intel); auto RO-Crate; FAIRSCAPE; Zenodo; Software Heritage |
| :---- | :---- |
| **License** | Apache 2.0 / MIT |
| **Source tabs** | Tabs 10, 19 |
| **Strong alternatives** | DVC (small data \+ Git), Pachyderm/ZenML/Flyte/Metaflow (alternative orchestrators), Nextflow \+ nf-prov (production genomics), Weights & Biases (cloud-tied) |
| **Status** | Decided. Six-layer architecture: bits, registries, runs, lineage, KG, archival. |

**TL;DR (30s).**

Reproducibility is a system property, not a virtuous afterthought. Every workflow run, every notebook execution, every model training job auto-emits a Workflow Run RO-Crate. LaminDB is the metadata fabric. Redun is the compute graph (the only mainstream Python orchestrator that hashes both code AST and data). Aim is the experiment tracker for a solo founder (\~200 MB RAM, 100K runs in SQLite). Zoekt (Apache 2.0, ex-Sourcegraph) plus GitNexus (MCP-native code KG via Tree-sitter ASTs and Leiden clustering on KuzuDB) replace Sourcegraph (which went private in August 2024). Tri-identifier coverage: DOI (Zenodo), SWHID (Software Heritage), ARK (FAIRSCAPE).

**Six-layer architecture (Tab 19).**

| Layer | Question | Tools |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **L6 Publication** | How publish/archive? | FAIRSCAPE · Zenodo · Software Heritage · RO-Crate |
| **L5 Reasoning** | How reason over artifacts? | LinkML · Biolink Model · BioCypher · Neo4j |
| **L4 Lineage** | How track lineage? | LaminDB · bionty |
| **L3 Compute** | How run computations? | redun · Aim/MLflow · Nextflow · Quarto |
| **L2 Registries** | Where artifacts live? | Six registries (data, models, code, papers, workflows, outputs) |
| **L1 Storage** | Where bits sit? | TileDB · S3/GCS · MinIO · Postgres · local FS |

**Compute graph: why redun.**

* Hashes both code AST and data. DVC hashes data only; Pachyderm, ZenML, Flyte, Metaflow use input fingerprint caching.  
* Existing Merkle-tree call graph maps directly to WRROC entities; redun RO-Crate plugin is the critical path artifact (Tab 19 Status: BUILD).  
* AWS Batch executor for redun is a 3-5 day build when scale demands.  
* Nextflow \+ nf-prov retained for production genomics workflows; redun general-purpose.

**Dataset versioning by scale.**

| Scale / domain | Pick | Why |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **\<100 GB mixed** | DVC \+ Git | Lightweight, HF import via dvc import |
| **PB-scale** | LakeFS | Graveler SSTables; diffs scale with change size |
| **Update-heavy** | Apache Hudi | ACID upserts at scale |
| **Single-cell** | TileDB-SOMA | CELLxGENE Census-compatible |
| **Neuroimaging** | BIDS 1.11.1 on filesystem | No TileDB bridge yet; bids-validator must pass |
| **Genomic variants** | TileDB-VCF | See Section 13 |
| **Tabular** | Parquet via LaminDB | Default for most tabular outputs |

**Models: serialization and zoo.**

* SafeTensors for shared weights; ONNX for cross-framework; GGUF for llama.cpp/Ollama edge.  
* Pickle is dangerous. Sleepy Pickle attacks demonstrated 89-100% scanner evasion in 2023\.  
* Local model zoo: MLflow's transformers flavor with save\_pretrained=False; LaminDB tracks artifact hash.  
* GGUF lacks native MLflow flavor; small contribution opportunity.

**Code intelligence after Sourcegraph went private.**

* Zoekt (Apache 2.0): trigram code search, Go binary or Docker, 30-minute setup, 3-5x source-size memory.  
* GitNexus: MCP-native code KG via Tree-sitter ASTs; Leiden clustering on KuzuDB; 7 MCP tools (impact, detect\_changes, rename, cypher, etc.).  
* Together they answer different questions: Zoekt is "where is this string?"; GitNexus is "what would break if I change this?". Both run locally; both expose machine APIs.

**Experiment tracking comparison.**

| Tool | License | Footprint | Best for | Status |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Aim** | Apache 2.0 | 200-500 MB RAM; 100K runs SQLite | Solo / small team, OSS-only | Pick (default) |
| **MLflow** | Apache 2.0 | Heavier; full server | Team \>3, MCP server in 3.4+ | Alt |
| **Weights & Biases** | Cloud-tied | Hosted, expensive at scale | Closed, cloud-only | Drop |
| **ClearML** | Apache 2.0 | Heavy ops footprint | Mid-size teams | Alt |
| **DVC tracking** | Apache 2.0 | Git-integrated lightweight | Small data, Git-native flows | Adjacent (data versioning) |

**End-to-end analysis flow (Tab 19).**

* 1\. Public source (CELLxGENE Census via TileDB-SOMA \+ private h5ad).  
* 2\. LaminDB ingest (ln.track(), hash \+ bionty validation).  
* 3\. redun workflow (preprocess, train, evaluate, AST \+ data hash).  
* 4\. Aim / MLflow tracking.  
* 5\. Auto RO-Crate (redun plugin emits WRROC).  
* 6\. Knowledge graph (BioCypher streams lineage to Neo4j).  
* 7\. Quarto paper (executable code chunks, Zotero MCP citations).  
* 8\. Publication (FAIRSCAPE ARK \+ Zenodo DOI \+ SWHID).

| Build backlog (Tab 19, 14-18 person-weeks).  BIDS-LaminDB wrapper (\~200 LOC), HF→MLflow/Aim mirror (\~200 LOC), Zotero RO-Crate bridge (\~100 LOC), LinkML artifact schemas \+ LaminDB→BioCypher adapter (\~300-500 LOC), redun RO-Crate plugin (CRITICAL), LaminDB RO-Crate exporter. The redun RO-Crate plugin is the single most important Cytognosis-internal artifact in the entire stack. Without it, no workflow run is FAIR by default. |
| :---- |

# **15\. Knowledge Management, Annotation, Whiteboarding**

**Surface: Spine**

| Primary pick | Hypothes.is (W3C WADM annotations) \+ Anytype (interim KM) \+ Zotero \+ Better BibTeX \+ Zotero MCP server \+ Mermaid \+ tldraw / Excalidraw |
| :---- | :---- |
| **License** | open / MIT (Hypothes.is, Mermaid, Excalidraw, tldraw); AGPL (Zotero); custom open (Anytype) |
| **Source tabs** | Tabs 7, 15 |
| **Strong alternatives** | Obsidian (Markdown KM, local-first; closed core), Logseq, Joplin, Affine, Reor, INCEpTION (semantic annotation) |
| **Status** | Annotation gold standard is W3C WADM via Hypothes.is. Anytype is interim until offline-first WADM-compliant KM matures. |

**TL;DR (30s).**

Hypothes.is is the canonical annotation tool because it implements the W3C Web Annotation Data Model natively. Memex (WorldBrain) had beautiful local-first UX but the storex backend is outdated and the schema is not WADM-compliant. Omnivore is dead (cloud service deprecated). Anytype is the interim offline-first knowledge workspace; LinkML classes map to Anytype Types. Zotero plus Better BibTeX plus Zotero MCP server is the reference manager (vector search, DOI lookup, open-access PDF cascade via Unpaywall, arXiv, Semantic Scholar, PMC). Mermaid is the default diagram language; tldraw and Excalidraw cover whiteboarding.

**Reference management.**

| Tool | Note | Status |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Zotero \+ Better BibTeX \+ Zotero MCP** | Library, citations, annotations; MCP server enables vector search and DOI lookup | Pick |
| **Papis (+ papis-zotero, papis-ask)** | CLI-driven reference manager; alternative | Alt |
| **JabRef** | BibTeX-focused | Alt |
| **ReadCube Papers, Paperpile** | Successful non-open examples | — |
| **100 LOC pyzotero bridge to RO-Crate** | Tab 19 build target | BUILD |

**Annotation: standards-first.**

* W3C Web Annotation Data Model (WADM): the only standard worth committing to.  
* Hypothes.is: gold-standard WADM implementation; multi-repo; PDF.js integrated.  
* Pundit: Linked Open Data based annotation.  
* INCEpTION: semantic annotation with external recommender via spaCy, SBERT, sklearn.  
* RecognitoStudio: niche; mentioned only.  
* Memex (WorldBrain): WADM-non-compliant; storex backend outdated. Drop.  
* Omnivore: cloud service deprecated. Drop.

**Knowledge management and note-taking.**

* Anytype (open repo): zero-knowledge encryption; offline-first; LinkML classes → Anytype Types. Pick as interim.  
* Logseq, Joplin, Affine, Reor: open repos worth tracking.  
* Obsidian: Markdown-only, local-first; closed core but mature ecosystem. Acceptable for personal notes.  
* Tana: non-open; interactive voice notes, super-tags as KGs (inspirational pattern).  
* MyScript: non-open; handwriting \+ math (inspirational pattern).

**Whiteboarding and diagramming.**

* Mermaid (+ live editor \+ zenuml-core): default for code-as-diagram in docs.  
* Excalidraw, tldraw, drawnix: open-source whiteboards; drop into Quarto via image embed.  
* Drawio, ZenUML, PlantUML: open alternatives for structured diagrams.  
* Pointless, CanvyDocs, Lorien, nextcloud/whiteboard: smaller open repos worth knowing.  
* Miro, Lucidspark, Microsoft Whiteboard, ClickUp: closed alternatives; not preferred.

# **16\. External Repositories Index**

**Surface: Spine**

| Primary pick | 16 catalogued GitHub organizations cloned into the Cytognosis development environment |
| :---- | :---- |
| **License** | Mostly Apache 2.0 / MIT / BSD |
| **Source tabs** | Tab 1 (Cytognosis External Resource Directory) |
| **Strong alternatives** | — |
| **Status** | Reference catalogue, not a recommendation tab. |

**TL;DR (30s).**

Sixteen external organizations are cloned and tracked as canonical sources of upstream truth. They cluster into five themes: Standards stack (GA4GH → Biolink Model → LinkML → OBO ontologies); Knowledge graph construction (BioCypher \+ Koza \+ KGHub \+ NCATS Translator \+ Biopragmatics); AI and agents (LangChain/LangGraph \+ BioContextAI \+ AI2/OLMo \+ BioChatter); Literature processing (GROBID \+ ScispaCy \+ OpenScholar \+ OLMoCR \+ OpenAlex \+ SemOpenAlex \+ CiteAs); Ontologies (OBOphenotype \+ Bioregistry \+ LinkML \+ Biolink Model).

**Catalogued organizations and their role.**

| Org | Role | Key repos |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **BioContextAI** | Biomedical MCP server registry. Cytoverse MCP exposure pattern. | anndata-mcp, knowledgebase-mcp, mcp-server-cookiecutter, meta-mcp, registry, skill-to-mcp |
| **BioCypher** | KG construction framework \+ BioChatter LLM interface. | BioCypher, BioChatter, Karenina, biolink-model, biolink-model-toolkit, kgx, ontobio |
| **GA4GH** | Genomic standards. | VRS, Phenopackets, DRS, Passports, CatVRS, VA-Spec, TRS |
| **Knowledge Graph Hub (KGHub)** | Disease-specific KGs \+ Alzheimer's agent. | kg-alzheimers, kg-idg, kg-obo, kg-phenio, agent-alz-assistant |
| **LinkML** | Schema language ecosystem. | linkml, linkml-map, linkml-owl, schema-automator, schemasheets, linkml-store, linkml-data-qc, linkml-term-validator |
| **Monarch Initiative** | Biolink Model \+ Koza \+ PhEval; HPO, MONDO, UPHENO ontologies. | — |
| **NCATS Translator** | Federated reasoner stack. | Babel, NodeNormalization, NameResolution, ReasonerAPI (TRAPI), Knowledge\_Graph\_Exchange\_Registry |
| **Allen Institute for AI (AI2)** | OLMo (open LLM), ScispaCy, OpenScholar, SERA, OLMoCR, codescientist, ai2-scholarqa-lib, dolma. | OLMo, OLMo-core, OLMoE, ScispaCy, OpenScholar, OLMoCR |
| **AWS Labs** | AWS MCP reference \+ open data registry. | mcp, open-data-registry |
| **Biopragmatics** | Identifier and ontology tooling. | Bioregistry, PyOBO, SeMRA, biomappings, biolexica, bioontologies, biosynonyms, bioversions, biolookup |
| **GROBID Organization** | PDF→TEI/XML extraction; \~0.87 F1 reference extraction; \~10.6 PDFs/sec. | — |
| **LangChain** | Agent framework family. | langchain, langgraph, langchain-mcp-adapters, deepagents, local-deep-researcher, open-canvas, open-swe |
| **OBO Phenotype** | Phenotype ontology family. | uberon, cell-ontology, HPO, upheno, brain\_data\_standards\_ontologies, ncbitaxon, provisional\_cell\_ontology |
| **Open Targets** | Drug target evidence; GraphQL platform. | gentropy, OnToma, platform-api, platform-etl-backend, open-targets-platform-mcp |
| **OurResearch / OpenAlex** | Bibliometric data \+ tools. | openalex-guts, openalex-concept-tagging, openalex-pdf-parser, citeas-api, total-impact-core |
| **Metaphacts / SemOpenAlex** | 26B+ RDF triples derived from OpenAlex; SPARQL endpoint; embeds DistMult. | metaphactory |

| How to use this index.  When a Cytognosis component asks "what's upstream of this?", check Tab 1 first. Each org maps directly to one or more component groups. BioContextAI feeds Multi-Agent Orchestration (MCP). BioCypher feeds Knowledge Graph. GA4GH feeds Genomic Data. AI2 feeds Foundation Models for Cytoverse and Cytonome edge. |
| :---- |

# **17\. Cross-cutting themes**

Seven themes thread through every component group. They are the operating principles that turn an inventory of tools into a coherent platform.

## 17.1 Open by default

Every primary pick is permissively licensed (Apache 2.0, MIT, BSD-3) unless explicitly flagged. AGPL exposure is limited to Zotero (where Better BibTeX and the MCP server are LGPL/MIT). Aligns with Cytognosis FRO mission and open-science deliverable language for ARPA-H, NIH, NSF, and EU Horizon Europe.

## 17.2 Schema is the hub

LinkML threads through document generation, the knowledge graph, lineage, and grant templating. Authors compile downstream artifacts (Pydantic, JSON-Schema, GraphQL, OWL, ShEx, JSON-LD, SHACL, SQL DDL, Markdown stubs, TypeScript types) from one source of truth. Avoid N-to-N mappings; they rot fast and Ahmen rot silently.

## 17.3 Local-first and privacy-preserving

Edge inference (Gemma 4 E2B on phone), on-device persona (Flutter \+ LiteRT-LM), CRDT state (Iroh Documents), local-first databases (DuckDB \+ WASM-SQLite, Postgres in Docker Compose, Anytype interim KM, Tauri WebView). PHI never leaves the phone except through schema-enforced filtered events.

## 17.4 Interoperability through MCP

Model Context Protocol is the de facto agent-to-tool standard in 2026\. BioContextAI is the biomedical registry. Cytognosis exposes Zotero MCP (Tab 19), MLflow MCP (Tab 10), GitNexus MCP (Tab 19), SemOpenAlex SPARQL (Tab 14), and BioCypher's Open Targets MCP. The supervisor LLM never imports custom adapters per source.

## 17.5 FAIR is operational, not aspirational

RO-Crate auto-emits from every workflow run (redun plugin is the critical path). Tri-identifier coverage: SWHID \+ DOI \+ ARK. CC0 metadata everywhere. CITATION.cff in every Cytognosis repo. The DMP is one master with four extracts (NIH, ARPA-H, NSF Tech Labs, EU Horizon Europe).

## 17.6 Dual-tier S2S, never single-process

Phone interviewer (Tier 1, 90% queries, edge-only) \+ cloud or laptop supervisor (Tier 2, 8%, 1-2s P95) \+ optional specialist (Tier 3, 2%, cloud only). Frameworks that require single-process execution (AutoGen, CrewAI, Letta, OpenAI Swarm, MAF on Orleans \+ MAUI) are out.

## 17.7 Reproducibility as system property

AST \+ data hashing (redun). Provenance binds code, data, model, paper. Quarto papers execute their own code chunks. Cookiecutter templates (Cytocast or Cytograft, name TBD) ensure every new repo starts FAIR-compliant.

# **18\. Phased roadmap, detailed view**

Five source roadmaps consolidated. Each track keeps its own internal cadence; the figure in Section 5 shows them on a single timeline. Below are the detailed deliverables per track and the explicit interlocks.

## 18.1 Foundation Spine (Tab 19, 6 weeks to working sandbox)

**Week 1: local tools**

* pyenv / conda; .env; accounts (GitHub, Zotero, Lamin.ai, HuggingFace, Software Heritage)  
* lamindb\[bionty\] \+ lamin init SQLite; tiledb-soma \+ cellxgene-census  
* redun \+ first @task; Aim wired to redun  
* Zoekt (Go binary) \+ GitHub org index; npx gitnexus@latest setup \+ 2-3 repos  
* Zotero desktop \+ Better BibTeX \+ MCP server; bids-validator on first BIDS dataset  
* Checkpoint: ingest data, run workflow, log metrics, search code (text \+ graph), Claude finds papers

**Week 2: shared infrastructure**

* Docker Compose with Postgres, MinIO, MongoDB, FAIRSCAPE \+ web client, health checks, backups

**Week 3: integration \+ RO-Crate MVP**

* Migrate LaminDB to S3/GCS \+ Postgres  
* Build redun RO-Crate plugin (Process Run Crate level) using ro-crate-py  
* Wire redun \+ LaminDB so every run lands in lineage

**Weeks 4-6: schema, KG, advanced**

* LinkML schemas: SingleCellDataset, BrainImagingDataset, NeuralNetworkModel, AnalysisWorkflow, Publication (1-2 weeks)  
* BIDS-LaminDB wrapper (\~3 days); HuggingFace mirror (\~3-4 days)  
* BioCypher LaminDB adapter (1-2 weeks); Zotero RO-Crate bridge (\~3 days)  
* FAIRSCAPE ARK \+ Zenodo deposition (\~3 days); AWS Batch executor for redun (3-5 days, when scale demands)

## 18.2 Cytonome MVP (Tabs 11, 12, 18, 8-10 months, $450K-650K all-in)

**Phase 0 (M0-1) Foundation**

* OpenAPI 3.1 v1 contracts; Style Dictionary design tokens  
* Identity provider chosen (Auth0 / Clerk / Supabase Auth / Keycloak)  
* CI pipelines on all three WebViews (Edge, Safari, Chrome via WebKit)  
* Flutter \+ Rive integration spike; Vite \+ React \+ Tauri shell \+ shadcn/ui  
* Supervisor: FastAPI \+ Google ADK \+ Postgres \+ pgvector \+ auth

**Phase 1 (M2-5) Vertical slice**

* Voice interview happy path (capture → transcript → next question)  
* Rive persona reactive states; encrypted local storage  
* Patient list \+ timeline \+ agent activity view \+ sidecar IPC  
* Interview-orchestration agent \+ Vertex AI deployment of Gemma 4 26B MoE  
* Milestone M5: end-to-end demo of one interview

**Phase 2 (M5-8) Capability \+ AI**

* Gemma 4 E2B \+ LiteRT-LM on-device, native audio input  
* Offline queue and sync; persona refinement  
* Cohort analytics; model evaluation views; agent config UI  
* Tauri sidecar with bundled Python supervisor  
* MedGemma 1.5 routing; RAG patient context; human-in-the-loop queue

**Phase 3 (M8-10) Compliance \+ ship**

* HIPAA audit, encryption review, audit logging, pen testing  
* WCAG 2.2 AA accessibility audit  
* TestFlight beta \+ Play Store; Tauri installers signed for macOS, MSI for Windows, AppImage/deb for Linux  
* Load testing; BAA finalized with Google Cloud; runbooks; automated eval harness

## 18.3 Voice AI deep work (Tab 13, 24 months, $1.8-2.7M for Phases 1-3)

**Phase 1 (M1-9) Pilot, $700K-1.2M, 6-8 engineers \+ 2 clinicians**

* Stack selection \+ legal review (M1, 30 days)  
* Whisper \+ Gemma 4 E4B \+ Kokoro (M2-4, 60 days)  
* Paralinguistic sensor (M1-5, 120 days): HuBERT-large SER \+ openSMILE eGeMAPSv02 \+ jitter/shimmer/HNR \+ filled-pause detection  
* Crisis detection v1 (M1-7, 180 days): hard-coded rules \+ multi-modal classifier \+ 988 handoff \+ warm clinician handoff  
* Clinical advisory \+ IRB (M1-4, 90 days)  
* Pilot deploy 100-500 users (M7-9, 60 days)

**Phase 2 (M9-15) Validate, $500K-800K**

* Clinical evaluation framework (90 days)  
* Crisis detection v2 (120 days)  
* Production pipeline \+ HIPAA cloud (180 days)

**Phase 3 (M15-21) Scale, $400K-600K**

* Migrate edge to Gemma 4 E4B (90 days)  
* Migrate ASR to E4B unified (60 days)  
* Scale to 10K+ users (90 days)

**Phase 4 (M21-24+, optional) Optimize, $400K-700K**

* Evaluate Moshi for naturalism (60 days)  
* Migrate to native S2S if ROI justifies (120 days)

## 18.4 Multi-Agent runtime hardening (Tab 11, 12 weeks → MVP, \+18-24 months HIPAA)

**Phase 1 (W1-2) Foundation**

* Ollama \+ LangGraph laptop, llama.cpp phone, latency on home LAN

**Phase 2 (W3-4) Discovery \+ transport**

* mDNS announcer/resolver, Tailscale or Nebula, NATS \+ JetStream

**Phase 3 (W5-7) Orchestration**

* LangGraph supervisor with tier routing, Iroh CRDT for session state, Cloud Ray \+ Claude API

**Phase 4 (W8-10) Reliability**

* TCP-like handshake gates, session checkpoint and replay, chaos testing

**Phase 5 (W11-12) Polish \+ demo**

* UI, observability, documentation; first end-to-end therapy interview demo

**Phase 6 (M4-9, optional) Healthcare hardening**

* PHI sanitization pipeline, HIPAA-compliant infrastructure, SOC 2 Type II prep

## 18.5 Doc generation pipeline (Tabs 2-5, 5-week migration \+ first grant cycle)

**Week 1**

* Install Docling \+ markitdown; A/B vs LlamaParse

**Week 2**

* Stand up Dockerized GROBID; pipe PI publication corpus

**Week 3**

* spaCy \+ scispacy \+ en\_core\_sci\_lg \+ en\_ner\_bc5cdr\_md

**Week 4**

* Port recalc.py from Anthropic skill

**Week 5**

* Adopt unpack.py / pack.py fallback for non-templated DOCX

**M3-9: First grant cycle**

* NIH new 7-element format (mandatory May 25, 2026\)  
* ARPA-H milestone-driven flexible format  
* NSF Tech Labs (Research.gov DMP tool launches April 27, 2026\)

## 18.6 Cytoverse foundations (Tabs 1, 6-8, 14\)

**M1-6 Pan-UKBB / PGC / ENIGMA harmonization**

* TileDB-VCF intake, GA4GH VRS hashing, BCF transfer  
* Hail VDS for distributed ETL on 1M+ samples  
* GWASLab or MungeSumstats for sumstats harmonization

**M6-9 First foundation model**

* TileDB-ML stream into PyTorch; first cell-state foundation model  
* BioCypher Neo4j v0.5 with 5 disorders

**M9-18 Cytoverse v1.0**

* 10+ disorders; production GraphQL API  
* MCP exposure for supervisor LLM in Cytonome

**M18-24 WGS \+ Cytoscope coupling**

* WGS integration; first Cytoscope biosensor data ingest

# **19\. Risks and open questions**

Consolidated from all source tabs. Severity uses the source tabs' own classifications (CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW). "Open" means the decision is unresolved as of 2026-04-26.

| Severity | Risk / question | Mitigation or status | Component |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **CRITICAL** | Crisis detection in voice agent | No open S2S model has built-in suicide / self-harm classification. Build hard-coded rules \+ multi-modal classifier \+ 988 handoff in Phase 1\. Cannot defer. | Voice AI |
| **CRITICAL** | Privacy boundary as code | Currently a verbal agreement. Must become a LinkML-validated schema with CI gates blocking any new field landing on the wire side without an explicit redaction policy. Precedes Phase 1 implementation. | Multi-Agent |
| **CRITICAL** | redun RO-Crate plugin | Single most important Cytognosis-internal artifact in the entire Spine. Without it, no workflow run is FAIR by default. Estimated 1-2 week build. | Reproducibility |
| **CRITICAL** | HIPAA non-compliance of distributed components | IPFS, Autonomi, and any public distributed storage conflict with right-to-be-forgotten. Centralize PHI in HIPAA cloud database. | Multi-Agent |
| **HIGH** | Mid-session connection drop | WebRTC alone has 15-20% failure rate. Mitigate with TCP-like handshake gates and 2-minute reconnect window via Iroh CRDT. | Multi-Agent |
| **HIGH** | Latency disrupts therapeutic alliance | Sub-300ms first-audio target; 600-800ms tolerable in Phase 1 with mitigations (filler audio, persona acknowledgment). | Voice AI |
| **HIGH** | Gemma 4 E2B requires ≥8GB RAM | Excludes budget Android \<$300. Decide hardware floor at M4: iPhone 14+ / Pixel 7+ vs broader Android with cloud fallback. | UI |
| **HIGH** | MedGemma 1.5 still on Gemma 3 architecture | Position Cytonome as preventive / behavioral, not diagnostic. Migrate when MedGemma 2 lands. Feature-flag pattern locked at Week 5-6. | Voice AI |
| **MEDIUM** | Prompt injection via A2A / MCP | Mitigate with mTLS \+ signed code (Dapr provides both). | Multi-Agent |
| **MEDIUM** | Data residency / PHI fragmentation | Audit which CRDT fields touch which jurisdictions before international rollout. | Multi-Agent |
| **MEDIUM** | Operational complexity | 5-6 complementary technologies in the Cytonome stack. Mitigate with bounded contexts and ownership matrix. | Multi-Agent |
| **MEDIUM** | Scaling laptop supervisor beyond 10 users | Cloud supervisor failover required at user count \> 10 simultaneous interviews. Architecture A.2 path planned. | Multi-Agent |
| **MEDIUM** | TileDB-BioImaging does not support BIDS | Substantial wrapper project. Defer until neuroimaging arm justifies. Keep BIDS on filesystem in the meantime. | Genomic Data |
| **MEDIUM** | FAIRSCAPE heavy dependencies | 2-4 GB RAM minimum. Fall back to pure RO-Crate \+ Zenodo if painful at Cytognosis solo-founder scale. | Reproducibility |
| **LOW** | Aim has small ecosystem (\~600 stars) | Mitigate by keeping MLflow as the team-scale alternative. Both work; Aim primary. | Reproducibility |
| **OPEN** | UI unification posture | Resolved in Tab 18: keep split (Flutter \+ React/Tauri). Revisit at v2 if brand parity demands single framework. | UI |
| **OPEN** | Aim vs MLflow split | Solo founder uses Aim. MLflow becomes primary when team exceeds 3\. | Reproducibility |
| **OPEN** | Cookiecutter template name | Cytocast vs Cytograft. Pick at first repo creation. | Reproducibility |
| **OPEN** | FDA classification self-assessment | M6 deadline. Determines whether MedGemma stays feature-flagged or replaced. | Voice AI / Cytonome |
| **OPEN** | Identity provider choice | M1 deadline. Auth0 vs Clerk vs Supabase Auth vs Keycloak. Single provider across Flutter \+ React. | UI |
| **OPEN** | NIH 7-element DMP format | Mandatory May 25, 2026 (NOT-OD-26-046). Master DMP must be ready as 7-section FAIR-organized. | Standards / FAIR |
| **OPEN** | Agent protocol fragmenting | MCP \+ A2A \+ ACP \+ ANP \+ LMOS. Watch list. Pick MCP \+ A2A; defer ACP/ANP/LMOS until industry consolidates. | Multi-Agent |

# **20\. Gaps to build (Cytognosis-internal artifacts)**

The 19 source tabs identify a small set of Cytognosis-specific artifacts that must be built rather than adopted from upstream. They are listed with effort estimates from the source tabs.

| Artifact | Effort | Notes | Source tab |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **redun RO-Crate plugin** | 1-2 weeks | CRITICAL. Auto-emits Workflow Run RO-Crate from every redun call graph. | Tab 19 |
| **LaminDB RO-Crate exporter** | \~1 week | Exports any LaminDB artifact tree as RO-Crate. | Tab 19 |
| **LaminDB → BioCypher adapter** | 2-3 weeks | Streams lineage into Neo4j; required for KG over runs. | Tab 19 |
| **BIDS-LaminDB wrapper** | \~3 days (\~200 LOC) | Validates and ingests BIDS datasets. | Tab 19 |
| **HuggingFace → MLflow / Aim mirror** | \~3-4 days (\~200 LOC) | Local model zoo registration. | Tab 19 |
| **Zotero → RO-Crate bridge** | \~3 days (\~100 LOC) | Pyzotero-based; turns Zotero collections into RO-Crates. | Tab 19 |
| **LinkML artifact schemas** | 1-2 weeks (\~300-500 LOC) | SingleCellDataset, BrainImagingDataset, NeuralNetworkModel, AnalysisWorkflow, Publication. | Tab 19 |
| **Privacy-boundary LinkML schema** | 1 week | Defines what may cross the wire from phone to laptop / cloud. | Tabs 11, 12 |
| **Crisis detection v1** | 6-12 months | Hard-coded rules \+ multi-modal classifier \+ warm handoff \+ 988 hotline. | Tab 13 |
| **Paralinguistic sensor** | 3-6 months | HuBERT-large SER \+ openSMILE eGeMAPSv02 \+ jitter/shimmer/HNR \+ filled-pause detection. | Tab 13 |
| **Long-form audio chunker** | \~1 week | Rolling 5s overlap \+ stitched context buffer to handle 45-60 min sessions. | Tab 13 |
| **redun → AWS Batch executor** | 3-5 days | When local compute exceeds capacity. | Tab 19 |
| **Cookiecutter template (Cytocast / Cytograft)** | \~1 week | Standardizes new repo structure with FAIR baseline. | Tab 19 |
| **LinkML → docxtpl context adapter** | \~1 week | Single function reused across grant funders. | Tab 2 |
| **3-tier HA pattern for supervisor** | 2-3 weeks | Application-layer; primary laptop \+ cloud hot-standby \+ on-phone degraded mode at 5s lapse. | Tab 12 |
| **OpenAPI 3.1 codegen pipeline** | \~1 week | Generates Dart (mobile) and TypeScript (desktop) clients from one spec. | Tab 18 |
| **Style Dictionary design token pipeline** | \~3 days | Compiles to Flutter ThemeData and Tailwind variables. | Tab 18 |
| **Sidecar IPC contract** | \~1 week | Tauri sidecar wraps Python ADK supervisor; same OpenAPI as cloud fallback. | Tab 18 |
| **Pipeline LinkML extraction (CGT)** | Ongoing | Cytognosis Genetic Intake pipeline (Stages 1-4 from Tab 8\) compiled to LinkML schema. | Tab 8 |

# **Appendix B. Glossary**

| Term | Definition |
| :---- | :---- |
| **ARK** | Archival Resource Key; persistent identifier for digital objects (FAIRSCAPE). |
| **Biolink Model** | LinkML-authored biomedical KG ontology; canonical entity and edge types. |
| **BioCypher** | KG construction framework (Adapters \+ schema\_config.yaml \+ Ontology Integration); native Neo4j bulk import. |
| **Cat-VRS** | Categorical Variation Representation Specification (GA4GH). |
| **CRAM** | Compressed reference-aligned sequencing format; \>30% smaller than BAM. |
| **CRDT** | Conflict-free Replicated Data Type; basis of Iroh Documents. |
| **Cytoverse** | Cytognosis component: AI map of continuous health coordinates (multi-omic foundation models). |
| **Cytoscope** | Cytognosis component: programmable biosensors for continuous biomarker tracking. |
| **Cytonome** | Cytognosis component: on-device causal AI navigator (voice interviewer, edge inference). |
| **Docling** | IBM-led multi-format document parser (MIT). Replaces LlamaParse. |
| **FAIRSCAPE** | Self-hosted FAIR research object archive with ARK identifiers. |
| **GA4GH** | Global Alliance for Genomics and Health; governs VRS, Phenopackets, DRS, TRS, Passports. |
| **GGUF** | Quantized LLM format used by llama.cpp, Ollama, LM Studio. |
| **GROBID** | Scientific PDF → TEI/XML extractor; CRF \+ DL; 0.90+ F1. |
| **HPO** | Human Phenotype Ontology; OBO Foundry. |
| **Hypothes.is** | W3C Web Annotation Data Model (WADM) implementation; gold standard for collaborative annotation. |
| **Iroh Documents** | Mobile-friendly CRDT distributed state; MIT. |
| **LaminDB** | Lineage-native lakehouse with bionty ontology validation; production at Pfizer, Altos, DZNE. |
| **LinkML** | Linked data Modeling Language; YAML schema source of truth. |
| **LiteRT** | Mobile NPU inference runtime (formerly TensorFlow Lite, FlatBuffers). |
| **LiteRT-LM** | LiteRT large-model loader; runs Gemma 4 E2B/E4B on-device with native audio. |
| **MCP** | Model Context Protocol; de facto agent-to-tool standard. |
| **MAF** | Microsoft Agent Framework on Orleans \+ MAUI (rejected for Cytonome). |
| **Neo4j** | Property graph database; Cypher query language; community edition adopted. |
| **ONNX** | Open Neural Network Exchange; cross-platform inference bridge. |
| **OBO Foundry** | Open Biological and Biomedical Ontology library (HPO, MONDO, UBERON, CL, etc.). |
| **openSMILE** | Open-source acoustic feature extractor; eGeMAPSv02 set. |
| **Quarto** | Pandoc-based scientific publishing system; Typst backend for fast PDF compile. |
| **RAIL-M** | Responsible AI License (Marker); license risk for Cytognosis open-stack policy. |
| **redun** | Insitro's Python orchestrator; AST \+ data hashing for true intelligent recomputation. |
| **RO-Crate** | Research Object Crate; portable, FAIR-aligned packaging for code, data, runs, papers. |
| **Safetensors** | Pickle-replacement weight format; zero-copy mmap; HuggingFace standard. |
| **scispacy** | Biomedical NER spaCy pipeline; UMLS, MeSH, RxNorm linking. |
| **SSSOM** | Simple Standard for Sharing Ontological Mappings (skos:exactMatch \+ curator \+ confidence). |
| **SemOpenAlex** | 26B+ RDF triples derived from OpenAlex; SPARQL endpoint by Metaphacts. |
| **SWHID** | Software Heritage Identifier (ISO 18670:2025); content-addressed code identifier. |
| **Tauri v2** | Rust-based cross-platform desktop framework; 30x lighter than Electron. |
| **TileDB** | Multi-dimensional array DB (TileDB-VCF, TileDB-SOMA, TileDB-BioImaging, TileDB-ML). |
| **VRS** | Variation Representation Specification (GA4GH); cryptographic hash variant identity. |
| **WADM** | Web Annotation Data Model (W3C). |
| **WRROC** | Workflow Run RO-Crate; profiles: Process Run Crate, Workflow Run Crate, Provenance Run Crate. |
| **Zoekt** | Apache 2.0 trigram code search; replaces Sourcegraph (which went private Aug 2024). |

| **Schema tooling** | Pydantic | P | 2, 3, 5, 18 | Python validation |
| **Schema tooling** | LinkML-Map | P | 7, 14 | Source-target mapping |
| **Schema tooling** | Schema-Automator | P | 7, 14 | Schema inference |
| **Schema tooling** | Biolink Model Toolkit (BMT) | P | 14 | Programmatic interaction |
| **Schema tooling** | Data Discovery Engine (DDE) | P | 14 | Bioschemas registry |
| **Schema tooling** | OAK (Ontology Access Kit) | P | 7, 14 | Ontology Python |

# **Appendix D. Cross-references and contradictions**

Resolved during harmonization. Use this appendix when a source tab seems to disagree with another.

## D.1 Cross-references

| From → To | Type | Detail |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Tab 4 → Tab 3** | Supersedes | Tab 4 references Templating Stack v2 as authoritative. |
| **Tab 5 → Tabs 3, 4** | Synthesizes | Tab 5 is the consolidated narrative version of templating research. |
| **Tab 6 → Tabs 7, 14** | Evolves | Three iterations of bioinformatics/SemWeb tab; each adds depth. |
| **Tab 7 → Tab 6, 14** | Adds desktop layer | Tauri \+ DuckDB \+ Hypothes.is desktop architecture. |
| **Tab 11 → Tab 12** | Refined by | Tab 12 refines Tab 11 with Gemma 4 \+ Dapr replacing llama.cpp \+ LangGraph supervisor. |
| **Tabs 11, 12 → Tab 17** | Evaluated against | Tab 17 evaluates SCION \+ LiveKit against Architecture A. |
| **Tab 12 → Tab 18** | Resolves UI tension | Tab 18 resolves Tab 12's open Decision 1 (UI unified vs split). |
| **Tab 12 → Tab 13** | Same model family | Both pick Gemma 4 family; Tab 13 adds depth on edge speech models. |
| **Tab 13 → Tab 18** | Voice models inform UI | Gemma 4 E2B native audio drives Flutter \+ LiteRT-LM choice. |
| **Tab 8 → Tab 19** | TileDB-SOMA | Tab 19 references Tab 8's TileDB ecosystem; flags BIDS gap. |
| **Tab 1 → Tabs 6, 7, 14** | Source repos | All KG/SemWeb tabs draw from Tab 1's catalog. |
| **Tab 9 → Tabs 13, 19** | Model formats | Safetensors \+ GGUF \+ ONNX apply to edge models and reproducible research. |
| **Tab 10 → Tab 19** | RO-Crate | Tab 10 ops guide; Tab 19 research ecosystem; both deploy RO-Crate. |
| **Tab 14 → Tabs 1, 6, 7** | Comprehensive | Tab 14 is the consolidated prose of Tabs 6+7. |
| **Tab 15 → Tabs 14, 19** | Catalogue | Tab 15's open-source list overlaps heavily with Tabs 14, 19\. |
| **Tab 16 → Tabs 10, 19** | FAIR utility list | Tab 16 quick list cross-refs DagsHub, Netron, DVC, MLflow, Aim, FAIRSCAPE, RO-Crate. |

## D.2 Contradictions resolved

| Topic | What the source tabs said | Resolution |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **LlamaParse** | Tab 2 (v1) recommends; Tabs 3, 4 (v2) replace with Docling. | Resolved: use Docling. |
| **Marker** | Tab 5 lists as alternative; Tabs 3, 4 defer (RAIL-M license). | Defer until license clarifies. |
| **Sourcegraph** | Tab 16 lists; Tab 19 notes private since Aug 2024\. | Replaced by Zoekt \+ GitNexus. |
| **MinIO** | Tab 16 lists; flagged deprecated, replaced by AIStor Free. | Use AIStor Free. |
| **MLflow vs Aim** | Tab 10 deploys MLflow as primary; Tab 19 recommends Aim for solo founder. | Aim primary; MLflow when team \> 3\. |
| **MAF on Orleans \+ MAUI** | Tab 12 lists as alternate; ruled out by Flutter mobile pick. | Dapr Agents wins. |
| **UI unified vs split** | Tab 12 leaves open; Tab 18 resolves: keep split. | Two surfaces, one product. |
| **LiveKit Agents** | Tab 12 sees as complementary signal path; Tab 17 rejects from core. | Optional Architecture A.2 cloud voice fallback only. |
| **PyMuPDF / fitz** | Tabs 3, 4 reject (AGPL); not mentioned in Tab 14\. | Universally rejected. |
| **xlsxwriter vs openpyxl** | Tabs 3, 4 reject xlsxwriter; both Anthropic and K-Dense converge on openpyxl. | openpyxl wins. |

# **Appendix E. Notes on sources and methodology**

Source: All Things — Tools (Google Doc), document ID 1yF-zW-TE6CbYfI8JGFJUvNOBKd-OAwhc3S09e8NqBwc, approximately 491,000 characters across 19 distinct tabs and \~7,500 lines, last extracted 2026-04-26.

**Method.**

* Read the full source document and extracted a structured outline with line-number citations.  
* Designed an 11-component taxonomy mapping all 19 tabs, organized across three platform surfaces (Cytonome, Cytoverse, Foundation Spine).  
* Generated nine visualization assets via matplotlib using the Cytognosis fluorescence-microscopy palette: architecture diagram, recommended-stack summary, maturity heatmap, four decision trees, consolidated phased roadmap, and tool-inventory donut.  
* Resolved ten cross-tab tensions and contradictions explicitly (Appendix D).  
* Consolidated five overlapping phased roadmaps into one parallel-track timeline with four critical milestones.  
* Validated FAIR alignment, openness, and Cytognosis fit on a heatmap covering 70+ tools.  
* Followed Cytognosis brand-identity rules: capital C for proper nouns (Cytoverse, Cytoscope, Cytonome), no em dashes, no "revolutionary / cure / breakthrough", "intercept" not "prevent", oxford comma, person-first language.

**What was added beyond the source.**

* Unified component taxonomy (the 11-component map; not in any source tab).  
* Recommended stack table consolidated to one page (Section 2).  
* Maturity / openness / FAIR fit / Cytognosis fit heatmap (Figure 3; new scoring).  
* Consolidated phased roadmap chart (Figure 9; new aggregation across five source roadmaps).  
* Risks and open questions consolidated to one severity-classified table.  
* Gaps to build consolidated to one effort-estimated table.  
* Source-tab traceability matrix (Appendix A).  
* Glossary (Appendix B).  
* Cross-references and contradictions resolved (Appendix D).

**What was preserved unchanged.**

* Every primary tool recommendation matches the source tabs exactly.  
* Every dropped or rejected tool is dropped for the reason stated in the source.  
* Every phase boundary and budget figure is taken from the source roadmaps.  
* Every open question is left open.

**Limitations.**

* The Cytoscope (biosensor) component is acknowledged in the architecture diagram but is largely out of scope for this tools-focused document; refer to the Cytognosis science-platform skill for that surface.  
* Some sub-sections in Tab 15 and Tab 16 are link catalogues rather than prescriptive recommendations; their content is captured but not over-interpreted.  
* The phased roadmap consolidates five source roadmaps; calendar-month boundaries are approximate.
